# ArXiv AI-Agent Papers — Cumulative Index

<!-- Auto-generated by .github/scripts/build-rxiv-index.py from triage/rxiv/data/*.jsonl. Do not edit by hand. -->

38 unique papers across 4 evaluated week(s).

## 2026 W25 (arxiv)

### [Your Mouse and Eyes Secretly Leak Your Preference: LLM Alignment using Implicit Feedback from Users](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20482)

- summary: This study introduces the IFLLM dataset to leverage implicit human feedback for improving the alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs).
- methods: reward model, DPO
- findings: Implicit user feedback boosts the accuracy of the text-based reward model from 55% to 64%.; The relative response quality improvements nearly triple after applying DPO to eight LLMs.; Users exhibit diverse types of gazing behavior and mouse trajectories.

### [What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20508)

- summary: This study investigates how different types of compliance demonstrations affect harmful compliance in language models, revealing that benign and harmful demonstrations interact in complex ways depending on the model and training methodology.
- methods: hypothesis testing, empirical analysis
- findings: Benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model.; Preference optimization is critical in preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance.; Demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias.; Models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning.

### [UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20474)

- summary: This study investigates 4-bit KV-cache compression for context-heavy agents, focusing on design choices and optimizations that enhance performance on AMD GPUs.
- methods: codebook quantization, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants, optimized decode-attention kernels, UltraQuant
- findings: UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in late rounds and 2.3x across all rounds; UltraQuant raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline

### [Sovereign Execution Brokers: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20520)

- summary: This paper introduces the Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB), a runtime enforcement boundary for managing certified authority in autonomous agents connected to cloud infrastructures.
- methods: runtime enforcement, certificate verification, policy checking, identity minting, API invocation, decision recording
- findings: SEB provides a mandatory enforcement point for certified authority; SEB separates proposal, admission, and execution; The prototype implementation shows measurable latency overheads and security under fault injection

### [S-Agent: Spatial Tool-Use Elicits Reasoning for Spatial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20515)

- summary: The study introduces S-Agent, a spatial tool-use agentic paradigm that enhances spatial reasoning over continuous multi-view images and videos by integrating evidence accumulation and temporal memory mechanisms.
- methods: spatio-temporal evidence accumulation, semantic planning, temporal memory mechanism, supervised fine-tuning
- findings: S-Agent improves both open-source and closed-source VLMs in a training-free manner.; S-Agent-8B significantly surpasses similar-scale baselines and performs comparably to advanced closed-source models.

### [Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20512)

- summary: This paper introduces probe-and-refine tuning, a method for improving LLM-based coding agents' guidance by using synthetic bug-fix probes to enhance repository context, demonstrating significant performance improvements over static knowledge bases and unguided approaches.
- methods: probe-and-refine tuning, synthetic bug-fix probes, single-shot LLM calls
- findings: Probe-and-refine achieves a 33.0% mean resolve rate compared to 28.3% for static knowledge base and 25.5% for unguided baseline.; Refined guidance produces evaluable patches for 14.5 percentage points more instances while maintaining constant per-patch precision (~59%).; Guidance allows agents to use a larger step budget productively.

### [Optimal Order of Multi-Agent and General Many-Body Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20485)

- summary: This paper presents a framework for analyzing multi-agent systems with feedback loops, focusing on agent power and response functions to derive macroscopic properties and optimize collective behavior.
- methods: framework development, macroscopic property derivation, system-level utility function analysis
- findings: Stronger synchronization can increase collective output but may also increase systemic fragility and reduce mobility.; Order, entropy, information, and useful energy are task-dependent and system-relative concepts.; Agent power distributions and response functions can be measured and designed to optimize collective behavior.

### [Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20517)

- summary: Multi-LCB is introduced as a new benchmark for evaluating large language models across twelve programming languages, addressing the limitations of LiveCodeBench (LCB) which is restricted to Python.
- methods: transforming Python tasks, contamination controls, evaluation protocol
- findings: evidence of Python overfitting; language-specific contamination; substantial disparities in multilingual performance

### [MemoryWAM: Efficient World Action Modeling with Persistent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20562)

- summary: MemoryWAM is a world action model designed for robust robotic manipulation that efficiently integrates memory and dynamics modeling to improve performance in non-Markovian environments.
- methods: hybrid memory design, tailored attention mechanism
- findings: MemoryWAM outperforms strong VLA and WAM baselines; MemoryWAM maintains favorable computational efficiency; MemoryWAM supports memory-dependent decision-making with reduced inference latency and GPU memory usage

### [Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20475)

- summary: This paper introduces Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA) to improve batch-style trace distillation by addressing cross-batch feedback inconsistencies and achieving superior performance across multiple benchmarks and models.
- methods: Marginal Advantage Accumulation, EMA, semantic identity merging
- findings: MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings; MAA consistently outperforms existing batch-level distillation baselines; MAA matches or surpasses online alternatives in most settings; MAA reduces optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%

### [LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20529)

- summary: The study introduces LedgerAgent, a method for tool-calling agents that maintains task states in a separate ledger to improve policy adherence and decision-making in customer service domains.
- methods: inference-time method, state management, ledger system
- findings: LedgerAgent improves average pass@k over standard prompt-based approaches; Largest gains observed under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics

### [HumanScale: Egocentric Human Video Can Outperform Real-Robot Data for Embodied Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20521)

- summary: This study compares egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data sources for embodied foundation models, revealing that egocentric data can lead to superior performance.
- methods: systematic study, filtering and labeling pipeline
- findings: Egocentric data can lead to superior performance compared to teleoperated real-robot data.; Models pretrained on egocentric data achieve a 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction.; Models pretrained on egocentric data have 52.5% and 90% higher success rates on in-distribution and out-of-distribution real-robot task execution, respectively.

### [GroundControl: Anticipating Navigation Failures in Vision-Language Agents via Trajectory-Consistent Uncertainty Estimates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20479)

- summary: The study introduces GroundControl, a trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator that improves the reliability of vision-language navigation agents by anticipating failure dynamics during execution.
- methods: constant-velocity Kalman filter, normalized innovation statistics, trajectory features, Selective Risk--Coverage Navigation (SRCN), risk--coverage curves, AURC, E-AURC
- findings: Trajectory-consistent uncertainty achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk.; GroundControl outperforms entropy-, conformal-, and heuristic baselines.; GroundControl consistently achieves the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and navigation splits.

### [Fast Human Attention Prediction for Fixation-guided Active Perception in Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20491)

- summary: This study introduces GazeLNN, a lightweight scanpath prediction model that enhances robot autonomy by efficiently modeling human visual attention.
- methods: scanpath prediction, auto-regressive architecture, feature extraction, Reinforcement Learning
- findings: GazeLNN requires only 0.61 GFLOPs.; Achieves a 0.47 ScanMatch score on the MIT Low Resolution dataset.; Outperforms existing recurrent baselines across diverse evaluation metrics.; Reduces computational costs by 99.40%.; Accelerates inference by up to six times.; Enables human-fixation-guided perception during autonomous navigation.

### [Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20537)

- summary: This study introduces execution-state capsules for low-latency, small-batch on-device physical-AI serving, enhancing responsiveness in interactive LLM agents and other systems.
- methods: graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism, snapshot and restore, forking and rolling back execution state
- findings: Capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode.; GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond.; TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens.; Recurrent state is load-bearing in contrast to KV-only ablation.

### [Efficient and Sound Probabilistic Verification for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20510)

- summary: This study introduces a framework for verifying security policies in AI agents operating in complex environments, addressing the limitations of existing deterministic approaches by incorporating probabilistic predicates.
- methods: runtime monitoring, distributionally robust optimization
- findings: Outperforms prior art; Improves security-utility trade-off; Ensures rigorous bounds on the probability of policy violation

### [Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20493)

- summary: This study introduces Contagion Networks to measure how biases from large language model evaluators propagate in multi-agent systems, revealing significant contagion effects and proposing strategies for mitigation.
- methods: controlled 3-agent experiment, Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix measurement, spectral radius analysis
- findings: Evaluator biases propagate consistently between agents with gamma in [0.157, 0.352].; Homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients.; Increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%.

### [Beyond Global Replanning: Hierarchical Recovery for Cross-Device Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20487)

- summary: The study introduces H-RePlan, a hierarchical replanning framework for multi-device agents that improves recovery from failures by separating device-local strategy recovery from global replanning.
- methods: hierarchical replanning, fault injection, cross-device workflows
- findings: H-RePlan outperforms single-strategy and coarse-grained multi-device baselines; achieves higher completion, instruction adherence, and perfect-pass rates; reduces token cost for reliable end-to-end success

### [Analyzing Defensive Misdirection Against Model-Guided Automated Attacks on Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20470)

- summary: This work analyzes the attack-defense dynamics in agentic AI systems using a probabilistic model and introduces a strategy called Contextual Misdirection via Progressive Engagement (CMPE) to mitigate prompt-injection and jailbreak attacks.
- methods: probabilistic modeling, detect-and-misdirect strategy, Contextual Misdirection via Progressive Engagement (CMPE)
- findings: Conventional detect-and-block defenses can lead to high attacker success rates as query budgets grow.; Detect-and-misdirect can reduce the positive predictive value of attacker-selected candidates.; CMPE reduces estimated attacker success rate upper bounds by up to two orders of magnitude.

## 2026 W24 (arxiv)

### [When Errors Become Narratives: A Longitudinal Taxonomy of Silent Failures in a Production LLM Agent Runtime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14589)

- summary: This study investigates silent failures in a long-lived personal-assistant LLM agent system, identifying a unique failure pattern and proposing a taxonomy and defense framework.
- methods: longitudinal study, root-cause postmortems, retrospective audit
- findings: 70% of silent failures were caught by human user-view observation, not tests or audits; 0% ex-ante prevention but 87% regression blocking found in retrospective audit of incidents; incident latency tracks failure mechanism, not code complexity

### [StreamMemBench: Streaming Evaluation of Agent Memory for Future-Oriented Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14571)

- summary: The study introduces StreamMemBench, a streaming benchmark designed to evaluate how personal-agent memory utilizes stored information and user interactions for future assistance.
- methods: benchmarking, experiments
- findings: Current memory systems often fail to use observed evidence.; Feedback is not reliably turned into follow-up behavior.; Four metrics diagnose evidence recall, initial evidence use, feedback incorporation, and follow-up reuse.

### [Spatially Conditioned Diffusion Policy: Learning Precise and Robust Manipulation with a Single RGB Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14535)

- summary: The study presents Spatially Conditioned Diffusion Policy (SCDP), a diffusion-based visuomotor policy that enhances manipulation tasks using a single-camera setup by leveraging end-effector trajectories as visual attention anchors.
- methods: diffusion-based policy, visual encoder, spatial conditioning module
- findings: SCDP outperforms strong single-view baselines; SCDP achieves performance comparable to multi-camera baselines; demonstrates precise manipulation and robustness to visual distractors

### [SIMMER: Benchmarking Latent Failures in LLM Executable Planning with a World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14574)

- summary: The study introduces SIMMER, a benchmark for evaluating latent failures in LLM planning in household environments, revealing significant shortcomings in current models and proposing a method to reduce these failures.
- methods: state machine executor, counterfactual foresight simulation
- findings: Even frontier models achieve at most 17% error-free plans.; Up to 56% of plans contain latent failures.; The majority of latent failures lead to irreversible consequences.; Explicit state reasoning can reduce latent failures by up to 72% and irreversible cases by up to 75%.

### [Safe Reinforcement Learning of Autonomous Highway Driving: A Unified Framework for Safety and Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14609)

- summary: The study proposes a unified safe reinforcement learning framework, MoE-RM-SRL, that enhances safety and efficiency in decision-making for autonomous vehicles by integrating safe distance, reward machines, and mixture-of-experts.
- methods: safe distance, reward machines, mixture-of-experts, deep Q-networks, sparsely gated MoE layer
- findings: MoE-RM-SRL improves safety and efficiency over state-of-the-art baselines.; The framework enables safe and reliable behavior without sacrificing efficiency.; The architecture mitigates instability and impulsive transients in control.

### [ORCA: A Platform for Open-Source Dexterity Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14561)

- summary: This work introduces the orca learning stack, an open-source research framework that unifies various aspects of dexterous robot learning to facilitate research in manipulation tasks.
- methods: low-level control, simulation, teleoperation, hand retargeting
- findings: The orca stack integrates control, simulation, and teleoperation behind a single interface.; It allows dexterous hand researchers to use the same data, training, and evaluation pipelines as non-dexterous robot learning.; A complete end-to-end workflow was demonstrated, including expert demonstrations, training an autonomous policy, and evaluating the learned policy.

### [Neither Parallel Nor Sequential: How DiffusionGemma Actually Commits Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14620)

- summary: This study investigates the decoding order of the DiffusionGemma 26B model, revealing that its token commitment is neither parallel nor block-autoregressive, but rather exhibits a partial left-to-right commit bias influenced by analysis granularity.
- methods: instrumentation, sampling, analysis of commit order
- findings: Decoding is neither parallel nor block-autoregressive.; Commit order is weak token by token but strengthens with coarser analysis.; Model commits in large simultaneous batches with undefined within-batch order.; Commitment is aggressive, finishing in a late burst within the step budget.; Task accuracy matches the autoregressive Gemma-4 sibling.

### [LoSoNA: A Benchmark for Local Social Norm Adaptation in Group Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14600)

- summary: This study introduces LoSoNA, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based agents' ability to adapt to local social norms in online group chats.
- methods: benchmarking, prompting conditions, evaluation of models
- findings: Naive prompting remains limited for most models; Explicit norm-aware prompting helps unevenly; Gemini 3.1 Pro reached 84.2% accuracy; Claude Fable 5 reached 81.6% accuracy

### [From Shield to Target: Denial-of-Service Attacks on LLM-Based Agent Guardrails](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14517)

- summary: This study reveals a novel vulnerability in LLM-based guardrails against prompt injection and jailbreak attacks, demonstrating how crafted data can exploit reasoning loops to execute denial-of-service attacks.
- methods: beam-search optimization framework, natural-language payload crafting, mechanism-aware structural mutations
- findings: Attack generalizes across diverse guardrail architectures and safety templates.; Payloads optimized on a single open-source surrogate transfer to eight leading model backbones.; Achieved 13--63× token amplification.; In real-world deployments, revealed up to 148× latency amplification.; A single poisoned document can saturate shared guardrail infrastructures.

### [Every Eval Ever: A Unifying Schema and Community Repository for AI Evaluation Results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14516)

- summary: The paper introduces Every Eval Ever, a community-crowdsourced repository and standardized schema for AI evaluation results to address inconsistencies in evaluation formats and frameworks.
- methods: community-governed metadata schema, automatic converters, crowdsourced community database
- findings: standardizes how evaluations are represented in a unified JSON document; source-agnostic design; enables fine-grained analysis

### [Code Correctness Signals in LLM Hidden States: Pre-Generation Probing and Repair Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14530)

- summary: This study investigates the legibility of code correctness in the hidden states of the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 model, revealing insights into the model's performance on code generation and repair tasks.
- methods: linear decoding, residualization, nested cross-validation
- findings: The correctness of the model's first-attempt code is linearly decodable from the prompt-final hidden state with a high AUC.; The hidden state shift from a failing attempt to its repair shows a statistically detectable contrastive direction, but this is influenced by repair-context covariates.

### [CARE: Controlling LLM-Generated Policies through Auditable Review of Evidence in Scientific Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14581)

- summary: The study introduces CARE, an auditable controller for optimizing high-throughput experimentation that balances LLM creativity with safety and stability.
- methods: auditable controller, public-evidence intervention gate, challenger ranking policies
- findings: CARE outperforms all other evaluated methods on Minerva/Olympus and ChemLex benchmarks; Final-best improved from 80.0 to 88.5 on Minerva/Olympus; Final-best improved from 83.9 to 92.1 on ChemLex; LLM self-evolution is more reliable under an auditable controller

### [BayLing-Duplex: Native Full-Duplex Speech Dialogue with a Single Autoregressive LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14528)

- summary: The paper introduces BayLing-Duplex, a native full-duplex SpeechLM that enhances real-time speech interaction in chatbots by eliminating the need for an external turn-taking module.
- methods: autoregressive LLM, fine-tuning, lightweight DPO stage
- findings: BayLing-Duplex achieves 92% turn-taking success and 100% interruption success on InstructS2S-Eval.; The speech-response score improved from 2.17 to 3.39 over Moshi.; BayLing-Duplex matches or surpasses its turn-based counterpart on various benchmarks.

### [AERMANI-PLACE: Language Guided Object Placement with Aerial Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14531)

- summary: The AERMANI-PLACE framework enables language-guided object placement for aerial manipulators by generating visual markers from natural language instructions and grounding them in the physical environment.
- methods: image editing model, depth observations, placement trajectory generation
- findings: The method achieved an average success rate of 87% on the test set for inferring placement locations from language instructions.; The approach transferred effectively to real-world aerial manipulation with an average success rate of 72%.

## 2026 W23 (arxiv)

### [Your UnEmbedding Matrix is Secretly a Feature Lens for Text Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07502)

- summary: This paper introduces EmbedFilter, a linear transformation that refines text embeddings from large language models (LLMs) by suppressing the influence of high-frequency tokens, leading to improved zero-shot performance on downstream tasks.
- methods: linear transformation, filtering high-frequency tokens
- findings: High-frequency tokens suppress the model's ability to capture nuanced semantics.; EmbedFilter enhances semantic representations by filtering out a latent subspace.; LLMs with EmbedFilter achieve superior zero-shot performance with reduced embedding dimensions.

### [Sparse Subspace-to-Expert Sharing for Task-Agnostic Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07500)

- summary: SETA is a framework that addresses the plasticity-stability dilemma in continual learning for Large Language Models by using adaptive sparse subspace decomposition into task-specific expert modules.
- methods: Mixture of Sparse Experts, adaptive sparse subspace decomposition, adaptive elastic anchoring, routing-aware regularization, unified gating network
- findings: SETA achieves competitive or superior overall performance relative to state-of-the-art continual learning baselines; SETA shows particularly strong retention of early-task knowledge; SETA improves backward transfer

### [How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07489)

- summary: This study examines how AI agents, specifically Perplexity's Computer product, accelerate and reshape knowledge work compared to traditional search tools.
- methods: empirical analysis, natural experiments
- findings: Computer performs 26 minutes of autonomous work per user session, compared to 33 seconds for Search.; Computer reduces completion time from 269 to 36 minutes on matched tasks, lowering estimated time and cost by 87% and 94%, respectively.; Computer queries more often cross occupational boundaries and require higher-order cognition.

## 2026 W22 (arxiv)

### [Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30353)

- summary: This study investigates the role of AI agents in research through a case study of a physicist supervising an AI coding agent to build a physics module, revealing critical insights into supervision practices and model limitations.
- methods: quantified case study, supervision events classification, oracle tests, parameter testing, changelog analysis
- findings: The AI agent resolved ten issues autonomously but struggled with root-cause resolution.; Supervision practices were critical for identifying issues that oracle tests missed.; The agent's inability to propose architectural alternatives limited its effectiveness.

### [DynaFLIP: Rethinking Robotics Perception via Tri-Modal-Dynamics Guided Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30350)

- summary: DynaFLIP is a dynamics-aware multimodal pre-training framework that enhances robot manipulation by integrating motion understanding into perception through image-language-3D flow triplets.
- methods: simplex-volume minimization, cosine regularizer, contrastive objective
- findings: DynaFLIP focuses on control-relevant regions critical for manipulation.; The resulting dynamics-aware representations serve as reusable visual backbones.; DynaFLIP consistently outperforms baselines across diverse downstream policies.; Gains reach +22.5% under out-of-distribution scenarios.
